response:
To derive the total query response time function $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) $ we must consider the distribution of queries among the three databases. Each database handles a proportion of the total queries. 

The total query response time is the sum of the response times of each database. For each database, the response time is a function of the number of queries it handles. 

The total query response time function is the sum of the response times of each database. 

The response time of MySQL is a function of the number of queries it handles. 

The response time of Oracle is a function of the number of queries it handles. 

The response time of Sybase is a function of the number of queries it handles. 

The number of queries handled by MySQL is $ \alpha Q $. 

The number of queries handled by Oracle is $ \beta Q $. 

The number of queries handled by Sybase is $ (1 - \alpha - \beta)Q $. 

The total query response time function is the sum of the response times of each database. 

The total query response time function is given by the sum of the individual response time functions. 

The total query response time function $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) $ is therefore the sum of the response times for each database. 

The response time for MySQL is $ T_{\text{MySQL}}(\alpha Q) = a \cdot (\alpha Q)^2 + b \cdot e^{c \cdot \alpha Q} $. 

The response time for Oracle is $ T_{\text{Oracle}}(\beta Q) = d \cdot \ln(\beta Q + 1) + \frac{e}{\beta Q + 1} $. 

The response time for Sybase is $ T_{\text{Sybase}}((1 - \alpha - \beta)Q) = f \cdot ((1 - \alpha - \beta)Q)^{\frac{3}{2}} - g \cdot \sqrt{(1 - \alpha - \beta)Q} $. 

The total query response time function is the sum of these three response times. 

The total query response time function $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) $ is given by the sum of the individual response time functions. 

The total query response time function is a function of the proportions $ \alpha $ and $ \beta $. 

The total query response time function is optimized by finding the values of $ \alpha $ and $ \beta
